say: All X, even if they don't have Y
think: We must preserve all records from table X. Standard left-to-right logic dictates table X is on the left.

say: X who have Y
think: Strict match. Only keep rows that exist in both tables.

say: Include X without Y
think: X must be fully preserved. Y is optional and can be NULL.

say: Keep all Y (where Y is the second table)
think: We need to preserve all records of the second table. We can use RIGHT JOIN or swap tables and use LEFT JOIN.

say: Return everything from both
think: Preserve all records from both tables. Unmatched rows from either side will be padded with NULLs.

say: Matching records only
think: Filter out unmatched rows from both tables. Standard intersection.

say: All combinations of X and Y
think: Cartesian product. Match every row of table X with every row of table Y.

say: Compare table X to itself
think: Join the table to a copy of itself. Requires distinct table aliases.

say: Active and inactive accounts
think: All accounts must be listed, regardless of active transaction records.

say: Only accounts with transactions
think: Strict match between accounts and transactions.

say: If department missing, show NULL
think: Employees is the primary table. If department is missing, we still want the employee.

say: Only assigned projects
think: Strict match. Exclude employees without projects and projects without employees.

say: All projects, even unassigned ones
think: Preserve the project table. Projects are on the Left or Right depending on join order.

say: Every combination of sizes and colors
think: No join condition exists; we want the Cartesian product of sizes and colors.

say: Employees and their supervisors
think: Hierarchy relationship in a single table. Match supervisor_id to employee_id.

say: Customers who haven't placed orders
think: Use LEFT JOIN, then filter where the order ID is NULL.

say: Products never sold
think: Left join products to sales, then filter where sale_id IS NULL.

say: Find siblings in database
think: Match person table to itself where parents match but person IDs differ.

say: Generate grid of store locations and dates
think: Cartesian product of locations and dates to build a reporting grid.

say: Complete union of employee and volunteer rosters
think: All employees and all volunteers, including those who are only one or the other.

say: Overlapping customers between regional databases
think: Only keep customers that exist in both regional tables.

say: Show all departments, along with employee counts (including 0)
think: Keep all departments. Left join departments to employees, group, and count.

say: Match on columns with identical names
think: Use NATURAL JOIN or USING clause for cleaner syntax.

say: Unmatched items from both sides
think: Retrieve rows that do not match in either table. Full Outer Join filtered with IS NULL.

say: All students and their test scores (including students who missed tests)
think: Preserve students. Left join to test scores.

say: Courses with or without registrations
think: Keep all courses. Left join to registrations.

say: Shippers and their orders (including shippers with no shipments)
think: If shippers is first, LEFT JOIN orders. If shippers is second, RIGHT JOIN.

say: Generate test matrix of users and test cases
think: Every user combined with every test case to evaluate coverage.

say: Find managers who report to other managers
think: Hierarchy query joining the manager/employee table with itself.

say: Match items with identical keys in both tables
think: Strict match. Exclude non-matching rows.

say: Keep Table A rows intact
think: Ensure every row in Table A (Left table) appears in output.

say: Keep Table B rows intact
think: Ensure every row in Table B (Right table) appears in output.

say: List patients who have never checked in
think: Left join patients to check-ins, then filter for NULL check-in IDs.

say: List empty rooms in hotel
think: Left join rooms to bookings (on active date), filter for NULL booking IDs.

say: Show items where manufacturer is optional
think: Keep all items; match manufacturer if details exist.

say: Display all users and their uploaded files, including file-less users
think: Keep all users; files table is optional.

say: Strict intersection of sets A and B
think: Return only rows that reside in both sets.

say: Full cartesian product of two tables
think: Multiply every row of A by every row of B.

say: Reconcile two bank registers (match or not)
think: Show all items from both registers to check for discrepancies.

say: Students in the same dorm room
think: Self-join students table on dorm_id where student_id != student_id.

say: Verify that primary key matches foreign key exactly
think: Classic inner join logic.

say: All orders, including guest checkouts without a customer record
think: Preserve all orders. If orders is on the right, use RIGHT JOIN.

say: Show all doctors and patients, regardless of appointment schedules
think: Preserve both tables. Show all doctors and all patients.

say: Find products priced higher than other products in the same category
think: Self-join on category with price comparison condition.

say: List departments and their assets (include assets with no department assigned)
think: Keep all assets. If assets is on the right, use RIGHT JOIN.

say: Match transactions using the 'account_id' column common to both
think: Can use USING(account_id) or standard ON clause.

say: Compare current year sales to previous year sales
think: Self-join sales table matching on product_id and year = year + 1.

say: Display all flights and airports (include airports with no flights and flights with no airport)
think: Preserve both datasets completely.

say: Show matching customer and order details
think: Strict match, discard unmatched rows.

say: Every employee matched against every project (to assign hours)
think: Cartesian product to list all potential pairings.
